_**Agent (composer-1)**_


Here are ways to squash all commits on a branch into one:

## Method 1: Interactive rebase (recommended)

If you want to squash all commits on your current branch:

```bash
# First, find out how many commits you want to squash
git log --oneline

# Then do an interactive rebase (replace N with the number of commits)
git rebase -i HEAD~N

# Or if you want to rebase from a specific commit/branch
git rebase -i <base-branch-or-commit>
```

In the interactive editor:
- Keep the first commit as `pick`
- Change all other commits from `pick` to `squash` (or `s`)

Save and close. In the next editor, edit the commit message if needed.

## Method 2: Reset and recommit

If you want to keep all changes but combine them into one commit:

```bash
# Find the base commit (the commit before your branch started)
git log --oneline

# Reset to that commit, keeping all changes staged
git reset --soft <base-commit-hash>

# Or reset N commits back
git reset --soft HEAD~N

# Now commit everything as a single commit
git commit -m "Your new commit message"
```

## Method 3: If merging into another branch

If you're merging your branch into another (e.g., `main`):

```bash
git checkout main
git merge --squash your-branch-name
git commit -m "Your new commit message"
```

Note: After squashing, if you've already pushed the branch, you'll need to force push:
```bash
git push --force-with-lease origin your-branch-name
```

Which method do you prefer? I can tailor the steps to your branch structure.

    Update .cursor/rules/architecture.md with any new information about the codebase from the changes you just made. Include:
- What changed
- Why it changed
- New patterns or conventions introduced
- Dependencies or relationships affected
- Any gotchas or edge cases discovered

Don't maintain it as a changelog. Instead, look for what was already present but has become obsolete now. Remove or update it as necessary. Look for what is missing and add it.

Keep the file organized and don't remove existing relevant context.
